region_of_origin: East Asia (China, Japan); widely cultivated across Asia

ontology.foodex2_description: Adzuki bean (Vigna angularis), raw whole seed

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of raw red beans is approximately 50 g (about 3 tablespoons of dried beans), which yields roughly 100–110 g after soaking and cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Red beans, mature seeds, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry F09020023
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record F09020023 (confidence 0.95). Extended micronutrient values (saturated fat, sugar, vitamins, minerals) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173735 for adzuki beans, raw, which is the closest approved-source equivalent. All values are consistent between the two sources within rounding tolerance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; adzuki beans, boiled (GI ~35, referenced as representative of raw dried adzuki/red bean class)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for raw (uncooked) red beans, as raw legumes are not consumed directly. The GI of 35 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed AJCN 2021 international GI tables for adzuki beans (boiled), which is the standard reference state for this food class. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × available carbohydrate per serving (50 g × 0.629 = 31.45 g) / 100 = 11.0, rounded to 11.1. This is the accepted convention for raw legume GI reporting.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (5 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (1254 mg/100 g) yield an outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 251:1, strongly supporting blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Red beans are highly suitable for hypertensive individuals; prepare without added salt and consume as a regular legume serving (50 g dry) to leverage their high potassium and fibre content for arterial health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) combined with high dietary fibre (12.7 g/100 g) and high protein (19.87 g/100 g) significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response despite high total carbohydrate content (62.9 g/100 g), consistent with ADA dietary guidelines for legume consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Red beans are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (50 g dry per serving); pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid sweetened red bean preparations that negate the low-GI benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.53 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high soluble dietary fibre (12.7 g/100 g), and high potassium (1254 mg/100 g) collectively support LDL reduction, arterial compliance, and cardiovascular risk reduction per AHA dietary guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of red beans as part of a plant-based diet is cardioprotective; their high fibre and potassium content support LDL cholesterol reduction and blood pressure management, and they are free of saturated fat and cholesterol at meaningful levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (12.7 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome diversity and immune regulation; polyphenols including anthocyanins in red beans have documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to RA management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red beans are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed cooked (boiling deactivates lectins present in raw beans); avoid sweetened preparations and ensure thorough cooking to eliminate antinutritional factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red bean, raw (also known as adzuki bean, Vigna angularis) refers to the dried, uncooked whole seeds of the small oval-shaped legume with a vibrant deep-red skin and cream-coloured interior. In their raw state, red beans are firm, dry, and have a mild earthy aroma. They must always be soaked and thoroughly boiled before consumption, as raw red beans contain lectins — heat-labile antinutritional proteins that are deactivated by boiling. Red beans are a staple ingredient across East and Southeast Asian cuisines, used in savoury dishes, sweet soups, desserts (such as red bean paste for mooncakes and tang yuan), and traditional beverages. Nutritionally, raw red beans are exceptionally nutrient-dense: per 100 g they provide 329 kcal, 19.87 g protein, 62.9 g carbohydrate, 12.7 g dietary fibre, and only 0.53 g fat with zero cholesterol. Their potassium content (1254 mg/100 g) is among the highest of any common legume, and their iron content (4.98 mg/100 g) makes them a valuable plant-based iron source. The glycemic index of cooked red beans is approximately 35 (low GI), making them suitable for blood glucose management. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, red beans are recommended for hypertension (high potassium, low sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fibre), and cardiovascular disease prevention (zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre). Their folate content (622 mcg DFE/100 g) is particularly notable for reproductive health and neural tube defect prevention.
common_user_queries: Are red beans good for diabetics?
What is the GI of red beans?
How much protein is in red beans?
Are red beans high in potassium?
Can I eat red beans if I have high blood pressure?
Are raw red beans safe to eat?
What is the difference between red beans and adzuki beans?
How many calories in red beans?
What nutrients are in red beans?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record F09020023 (confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Extended micronutrient values cross-referenced and supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173735 for adzuki beans, raw — an exact food match. GI value sourced from peer-reviewed AJCN 2021 international GI tables via University of Sydney GI Database for adzuki beans (boiled), the standard reference state. All values are internally consistent and supported by two independent approved sources.
notes: 1. Sugar_g reported as 0.0: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735) reports total sugars for raw adzuki beans as 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed zero per approved source. 2. Trans_fat_g reported as 0.0: naturally zero for an unprocessed plant-based legume; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. 3. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0: these are naturally zero or below detection threshold in raw dried red beans per USDA SR Legacy. 4. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA sources. 5. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on cooked adzuki beans (the standard reference state); raw uncooked beans are not consumed directly and have no direct GI measurement. 6. Potassium (1254 mg/100 g), calcium (66 mg/100 g), and iron (4.98 mg/100 g) values are sourced from HPB Foodo F09020023 and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values within rounding tolerance. 7. Raw red beans contain lectins (phytohaemagglutinin) that must be deactivated by thorough boiling; this food is not safe for direct consumption without cooking. 8. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the HPB source classification, which categorises legumes under this group as a plant-based protein alternative.
